algorithms.name: Next Greater Element
algorithms.analogy: Standing in a queue of people of different heights. Each person's 'next greater' is the first taller person to their right. A monotonic stack holds people still waiting to find someone taller. The moment you see someone taller, all shorter people in the stack finally get their answer.
algorithms.story: The monotonic stack is one of the most powerful interview patterns. It solves O(n²) brute-force comparisons in O(n) by maintaining a stack that is always sorted (decreasing for NGE). When you see a new element larger than the stack top, pop and record — that new element IS the next greater. Everything not yet popped stays in the stack, waiting.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Next greater/smaller element problems
• Stock span problem
• Largest rectangle in histogram
• Trapping rain water
• Daily temperatures (LeetCode 739)
• Any problem asking for the nearest larger/smaller to the right or left
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Initialize stack (stores indices), result array filled with -1
2. For each index i from 0 to n-1:
   a. While stack not empty AND arr[stack.top] < arr[i]:
      result[stack.pop()] = arr[i]  ← i is the NGE for the popped index
   b. Push i onto stack
3. Elements remaining in stack have no NGE (result stays -1)
4. Return result
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Store indices in the stack, not values — you need the index to fill result[index]
• Decreasing monotonic stack (pop when current > top) → next greater; flip to increasing (pop when current < top) → next smaller
• Circular arrays: loop twice (0 to 2n-1) using i % n as the actual index
• Daily Temperatures (LeetCode 739) is the same pattern — store days not temperatures, result[i] = i - stack.pop()
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Daily Temperatures (LeetCode 739)","desc":"For each day, find how many days until a warmer temperature. Monotonic decreasing stack of indices — result[popped] = i - popped."},{"title":"Next Greater Element I/II (LeetCode 496/503)","desc":"Classic NGE on array or circular array. LeetCode 496 maps values between two arrays using a HashMap."},{"title":"Stock Span Problem","desc":"For each day, how many consecutive prior days had price <= today? Equivalent to distance to previous greater element."},{"title":"Largest Rectangle in Histogram","desc":"Uses a monotonic increasing stack — each bar's NGE to left and right determines the maximum rectangle through that bar."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Storing values instead of indices in the stack — you need the index to update result[index]","Wrong comparison direction: < for next greater, > for next smaller — don't mix them","Not handling circular arrays: must loop twice and use i % n","Leaving elements in the stack at the end: they have no NGE — result stays -1 (already initialized)"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Next Smaller Element","desc":"Use a monotonic increasing stack: pop when nums[stack.top] > nums[i]. Result is the next smaller value."},{"name":"Previous Greater Element","desc":"Process right to left instead of left to right with the same stack logic."},{"name":"Circular Array Next Greater","desc":"Loop 0 to 2n-1, use i % n as array index. Only push to stack when i < n to avoid duplicates."}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Monotonic DECREASING stack → Next Greater Element (pop when current > top). Always store INDICES, not values — you need the index to fill result[index] and to compute distances. Amortized O(n): each element pushed and popped exactly once. Circular arrays: loop 0 to 2n using i%n.
algorithms.patternSignal: next greater element, next smaller element, daily temperatures, stock span, nearest larger, circular array, monotonic stack, previous greater
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Daily Temperatures","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Monotonic decreasing stack of indices. result[popped] = i - popped (days until warmer)"},{"name":"Next Greater Element I","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Build NGE map for nums2, then look up each element of nums1"},{"name":"Next Greater Element II (Circular)","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Loop 0 to 2n-1 using i%n. Only push when i < n to avoid duplicates in result"},{"name":"Stock Span Problem","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Equivalent to distance to previous greater — monotonic stack of [price, span] pairs"},{"name":"132 Pattern","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Scan right to left. algorithms.name: Valid Parentheses
algorithms.analogy: Every opening bracket is like opening a box. Closing brackets must close the most recently opened box — last opened, first closed. A stack naturally models LIFO. If you try to close a box that doesn't match the last opened, or the stack is empty when you try to close, the sequence is invalid.
algorithms.story: Valid parentheses is the canonical stack problem. Push opening brackets. When you see a closing bracket, pop and verify it matches. Empty stack at the end = all matched. This pattern extends to XML/HTML tag matching, code editor bracket highlighting, and expression evaluation.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Bracket/parenthesis matching in any form
• Balanced expression validation
• Remove minimum to make valid parentheses
• Score of parentheses
• Decode string (nested brackets)
• Calculator expressions
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Create map: ')' → '(', ']' → '[', '}' → '{'
2. For each character c:
   a. If c is an opening bracket: push c
   b. If c is a closing bracket:
      If stack empty OR stack.pop() != map.get(c): return false
3. Return stack.isEmpty() — all openers must be closed
algorithms.interviewTips:
• Use map.containsValue(c) or a Set of openers to detect opening brackets cleanly
• Pop then compare — don't peek then pop, stack.pop() is atomic
• Must check stack.isEmpty() before popping — closing bracket on empty stack = invalid
• Return stack.isEmpty() at the end — a non-empty stack means unmatched openers
• Follow-up: minimum removals (LeetCode 1249) — store indices, remove unmatched indices at the end
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Valid Parentheses (LeetCode 20)","desc":"The entry-level stack problem — tests understanding of LIFO matching and edge cases."},{"title":"Minimum Remove to Make Valid (LeetCode 1249)","desc":"Store indices of unmatched brackets, build result string excluding those indices."},{"title":"Decode String (LeetCode 394)","desc":"Stack-based bracket decoder: push count and current string on '[', pop and repeat on ']'."},{"title":"Basic Calculator (LeetCode 224)","desc":"Evaluate arithmetic expressions with parentheses using a stack for operator/operand management."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Not checking stack.isEmpty() before pop — closing bracket on empty stack causes NoSuchElementException","Forgetting stack.isEmpty() at the end — unmatched openers leave the stack non-empty","Using stack.peek() then stack.pop() separately — stack.pop() does both in one call","Checking containsKey with the wrong direction in the map — opening vs closing bracket lookup"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Minimum Add to Make Valid","desc":"Count unmatched '(' and ')' in a single pass — no stack needed, just two counters."},{"name":"Minimum Remove to Make Valid","desc":"Store indices of unmatched brackets in a stack and Set, then build result skipping those indices."},{"name":"Longest Valid Parentheses Substring","desc":"Stack-based O(n): push indices of '(' and unmatched ')'. Answer = max gap between consecutive invalid indices."}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Push opening brackets. Two critical checks: stack.isEmpty() BEFORE popping (unmatched closing = invalid immediately), and stack.isEmpty() AT THE END (unmatched opening = invalid). Return stack.isEmpty() — don't return true without this final check.
algorithms.patternSignal: balanced brackets, valid parentheses, matching delimiters, bracket expression, decode string, calculator parentheses, remove invalid brackets
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Valid Parentheses","difficulty":"Easy","hint":"Push openers. On closer: check empty first, then pop and match. Return stack.isEmpty()"},{"name":"Minimum Remove to Make Valid Parentheses","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Store indices of unmatched brackets, build result string excluding those indices"},{"name":"Longest Valid Parentheses","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"Stack stores indices. Push ')' indices that have no match, push '(' indices. Gaps = valid substrings"},{"name":"Score of Parentheses","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Stack tracks running scores. '(' pushes 0, ')' pops and either adds or doubles"}]

algorithms.name: Min Stack
algorithms.analogy: A stack of numbered boxes. You want to know the smallest number in the entire stack at any time. Instead of scanning all boxes (O(n)), you keep a shadow stack that records what the minimum WAS at each height. When you pop, you also pop from the shadow — instantly restoring the previous minimum.
algorithms.story: Min Stack is a design problem testing state synchronization. The auxiliary minStack mirrors the main stack but records the running minimum at each level. Push to both stacks simultaneously. Pop from both simultaneously. getMin() is always minStack.peek() — O(1) without any scanning.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Any design problem requiring O(1) min/max alongside standard stack operations
• Foundation for Max Stack, Stack with median
• Interview design round — commonly asked at FAANG
• Implementing monotonic data structures with auxiliary info
algorithms.howItWorks:
Maintain two stacks: main stack and minStack.
push(x):
  push x to main stack
  push min(x, minStack.peek()) to minStack
pop():
  pop from both stacks simultaneously
top(): return main.peek()
getMin(): return minStack.peek()
algorithms.interviewTips:
• The dual-stack approach is clean and always correct — explain it first
• Interviewer may ask for space-optimized: store pairs [value, currentMin] in a single stack (saves one stack object, same O(n) space)
• Follow-up: Max Stack (same pattern, track max instead of min)
• Follow-up: Stack with increment operation — each pop increments bottom k elements (use lazy propagation)
• Key insight: the minStack at position i stores the minimum of all elements CURRENTLY at or below position i
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Min Stack (LeetCode 155)","desc":"Classic O(1) all-operations design problem. Dual stack with synchronized push/pop."},{"title":"Max Stack","desc":"Same pattern but tracking maximum. Follow-up often asked in the same interview."},{"title":"Monotonic Stack Applications","desc":"MinStack concept generalises to tracking the maximum/minimum over a sliding window (deque-based variant)."},{"title":"Undo History with Min Cost","desc":"Track minimum cost state across a series of operations, allowing O(1) rollback to any previous state."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Forgetting to push to minStack in sync with main stack","Forgetting to pop from minStack in sync with main stack pop","Thinking getMin() needs to scan — it must be O(1) via the auxiliary stack","Edge case: single element — minStack should push the element itself as the current minimum"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Max Stack","desc":"Track maximum instead of minimum using the same dual-stack pattern. O(1) getMax()."},{"name":"Stack with Increment","desc":"Add increment(k, val) that adds val to bottom k elements. Use lazy propagation — track increments at positions, apply on pop."}]
algorithms.keyInsight: Two synchronized stacks: main + minStack. On every push, push min(val, minStack.peek()) to minStack. On every pop, pop from BOTH stacks simultaneously. minStack.peek() is ALWAYS the current minimum O(1) — it mirrors the state of the main stack at every level.
algorithms.patternSignal: O(1) min or max stack, design stack with min, auxiliary stack, synchronized data structure, constant time minimum, stack with extra info
algorithms.practiceProblems: [{"name":"Min Stack","difficulty":"Medium","hint":"Dual stack: push min(val, minStack.peek()) to minStack on every push. Pop both on pop"},{"name":"Max Stack","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"Same dual-stack pattern but track max. Follow-up: support popMax() — needs TreeMap"},{"name":"Maximum Frequency Stack","difficulty":"Hard","hint":"Track frequency of each element and a map of freq → stack. Pop from highest frequency stack"}]

algorithms.name: Largest Rectangle in Histogram
algorithms.analogy: For each bar, ask: 'If I extend horizontally at MY height, how far left and right can I go before hitting a shorter bar?' That gives the maximum rectangle using this bar as the height. A monotonic increasing stack finds the previous smaller and next smaller bar for EVERY bar in one left-to-right pass.
algorithms.story: One of the hardest stack problems. The key: each bar can be the HEIGHT of a rectangle extending left and right until hitting a shorter bar. Maintain a monotonic increasing stack. When you see a shorter bar, the top element has found its right boundary; the new top is its left boundary. Area = height × (right_idx − left_idx − 1). A virtual bar of height 0 at position n flushes the stack at the end.
algorithms.whenToUse:
• Largest rectangle in histogram (classic)
• Maximal rectangle in binary matrix (reduce each row to a histogram)
• Skyline problem
• Any problem finding the maximum area under a bar-chart constraint
algorithms.howItWorks:
1. Stack stores indices (maintains increasing heights)
2. For i from 0 to n (include virtual bar of height 0 at i=n):
   While stack not empty AND heights[stack.top] > heights[i]:
     h = heights[stack.pop()]  (this bar's height)
     w = stack.empty ? i : (i - stack.peek() - 1)  (width)
     maxArea = max(maxArea, h × w)
   Push i
3. Return maxArea
algorithms.interviewTips:
• The virtual 0-height bar at i=n flushes all remaining bars from the stack — elegant, avoids post-loop cleanup
• Width formula: when stack is empty after pop, the bar extends to the entire left side, so width = i; otherwise width = i - stack.peek() - 1
• This is the key trick most people miss: the -1 accounts for the fact that stack.peek() is the last smaller bar, not part of the rectangle
• Maximal rectangle in matrix: rebuild histogram row by row and run this algorithm on each row
algorithms.useCases: [{"title":"Largest Rectangle in Histogram (LeetCode 84)","desc":"Classic O(n) monotonic stack solution — the benchmark for hard stack problems."},{"title":"Maximal Rectangle in Binary Matrix (LeetCode 85)","desc":"Build running height histogram per row, apply histogram solution to each row. O(m×n) total."},{"title":"Trapping Rain Water (LeetCode 42)","desc":"Similar monotonic stack technique — find the left and right boundaries for each position."},{"title":"Skyline Problem (LeetCode 218)","desc":"Process building edges with a max-heap or monotonic structure to extract silhouette changes."}]
algorithms.pitfalls: ["Forgetting the virtual 0-height sentinel bar at position n — stack never fully empties without it","Width calculation: when stack is empty after pop, width = i (not i-1) — the bar spans the entire left portion","Width calculation: when stack is not empty, width = i - stack.peek() - 1, not i - stack.peek()","Confusing the index vs value stored in the stack (always store indices, not heights)"]
algorithms.variants: [{"name":"Maximal Rectangle in Binary Matrix","desc":"Treat each row as a histogram where heights[j] = consecutive 1s above. Apply histogram algorithm row by row. LeetCode 85."},{"name":"Trapping Rain Water","desc":"Two-pointer or monotonic stack approach. For each position, trapped water = min(leftMax, rightMax) - height. LeetCode 42."}]
algorithms.keyInsight: For each popped bar: HEIGHT = heights[popped], WIDTH = i - stack.peek() - 1 (or just i if stack empty after pop). Virtual 0-height sentinel at position n forces all remaining bars to compute area. The -1 in width formula accounts for stack.peek() being the last SHORTER bar — it's not part of this rectangle.
algorithms.patternSignal: largest rectangle histogram, maximal rectangle binary matrix, trapping rain water, monotonic increasing stack, area calculation, maximum area
